core_identity.title: Tangent Graphs: Balance, Limits, and Wellbeing
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Explain the behaviour of y = tan(x) using its definition tan(θ) = sin(θ)/cos(θ), identify its period and vertical asymptotes, and use y = a·tan(bx + c) + d to model and interpret threshold-like behaviours in context.

verse_ality.driving_question: How can the tangent graph help us understand rapid change, limits, and ‘danger zones’ in real systems?

knowing_world.focus: Learn how the tangent function arises from sin(θ)/cos(θ), why it becomes undefined when cos(θ)=0, and how this creates vertical asymptotes and steep repeating curves with period π.
key_concepts: Tangent as a ratio: tan(θ) = sin(θ)/cos(θ)
Radians and the unit circle link
Graph features: steep growth near asymptotes
tools: Interactive: Tangent from the Unit Circle (canvas_file_embed): https://canvas.instructure.com/courses/12822206/files/314020089/download
activities: Explore what happens to tan(θ) as θ approaches ±π/2.
Identify where the graph crosses the x-axis and connect this to sin(θ)=0.
Explain how the sketch shows undefined values and asymptotes.

human_story.focus: Use tangent-like behaviour as a way to think about systems that can surge rapidly toward unsafe thresholds (e.g., stress responses or health indicators), and how early warning signs can support fairer outcomes.
justice_prompts: In healthcare, where might it be helpful to know when a value is approaching a danger zone or ‘asymptote’?
Not everyone has equal access to preventative health tools. How can maths help highlight early warning signs and ensure care reaches those who need it most?

expressive_self.focus: Use metaphor and reflection to connect the idea of rapid spikes and ‘safe ranges’ to lived experience, building emotional literacy alongside mathematical insight.
activities: Describe a time when your mood or energy spiked quickly (metaphor-based reflection).
Sketch your ‘safe range’ and your personal ‘asymptotes’.
Reflect: When do you feel most balanced — and what throws you off?

designed_world.focus: Experiment with transformations of y = a·tan(bx + c) + d to represent threshold-like patterns, then interpret what the parameters mean in a wellbeing or biological model.
design_challenges: Adjust parameters to model heart rate under exercise vs. rest.
Model a stress response to workload.
Model a mental ‘overload’ pattern and describe what the asymptotes represent.

regulation_supports: Body prompt: Imagine your own tangent curve — what are your ‘safe zones’? When do you feel you’re rising too steeply?
Breath activity: Inhale slowly as arms rise overhead; exhale as arms lower; repeat with calm awareness of rhythm.

pathways.name: Final Task: Modelling with Tangent (SDG 3 – Good Health and Wellbeing)
pathways.focus: Design a tangent-based graph that models a real or metaphorical health pattern, using parameters and asymptotes to represent boundaries and threshold behaviour.
products: A graph of y = a·tan(bx + c) + d with labelled parameters and asymptotes.
A paragraph or audio reflection connecting the model to wellbeing and boundaries.
Optional: creative metaphor, drawing, or movement description.
criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Explains graph behaviour, transformations, and asymptotes.
Fluency & Accuracy – Correct handling of undefined values and steep growth.
Application to Problems – Uses tangent to meaningfully model a human or biological pattern.
Independence & Reflection – Thoughtful connection to real experience or SDG.

required: Basic understanding of coordinates and reading graphs
Comfort with radians and the unit circle idea (or readiness to learn it)
Understanding of sine and cosine (or access to prior lessons)
Using a calculator for π-based values where needed

builds_toward: Solving trig equations and interpreting restrictions
Further trig graphs and applications in contexts
